segments.text: Thank you for the opportunity to speak. Can you hear me?
Yes.
My name's William McGumsey. I'm a former policy advisor, having worked for several
government departments on a range of projects over 15 years, and I
now work in independent media.
My submission focuses on the immigration risks posed by the free trade agreement.
However, rather than opposing the agreement outright, as many who share my concerns
around immigration do, I think those risks can be addressed by
changes to general immigration settings.
Outside of the risks I identify, I support the FTA because it
diversifies our export markets, reduces our dependence on
China, and because there is significant upside potential for trade and
economic benefits as the Indian economy grows.
The debate over the FTA comes in a context when most of our traditional
allies are moving to tighten their immigration settings.
The reason for this tightening is that Europe, the US, the UK, and Australia
have in recent years all experienced growing immigration-related problems
such as increased frequency of terror attacks, rising migrant crime,
threats to social cohesion, national identity, and trust in
institutions.
In December 2025, the US released an updated national security
strategy with border security at its center.
The strategy pointed out the problems mass migration is causing in Europe,
weakened social cohesion, political fragmentation, reduced state
legitimacy, and strain on welfare and public services.
The strategy also said that NATO allies may, over time,
become majority non-European, which might change their political
alignment and reliability as US partners.
Here in New Zealand, our own Security Intelligence Service, in its August
2025 Security Threat Environment report, identified the
risk of foreign political interference via diaspora populations
as a key problem facing New Zealand.
This is
of particular relevance to the debate around the FTA, as India's
Prime Minister Modi is on the record about his desire to use the Indian
diaspora as a soft power tool to influence Western governments.
Here, I'd like to note the link between diaspora lobbying and hate speech
laws.
Often, diaspora populations, through their ethnic lobbying
organizations, are some of the biggest proponents of hate speech
laws. And while genuine hate and racial vilification can be
present, hate speech laws are often used as a tool to
suppress public discussion of the activities of these lobbying
organizations and the ethnic diasporas they represent.
To restate this, one of the reasons to support free speech and
oppose hate speech laws is to ensure we can have public
transparency about the activities of ethnic lobbying
organizations and the ethnic diasporas
when they run contrary to the common good or the interests of other parts of the
population.
I can pause there if you want to have time for questions, or I can continue on and
sort of restate some of the specific threats and mitigations
I point out about the FTA itself.
Any questions at this stage?
No. Carry on please, William.
Okay. So particular risks posed by the FTA.
Increased permanent residence applications.
So although the migration provisions in the FTA have been
intelligently designed to be temporary, it will still have the
likely effect of widening the funnel at the front end and allowing
more migrants to meet the criteria and apply for permanent residence.
This is the single most important thing we need to do to mitigate the immigration
risks around the FTA, and that is to tighten the
permanent residence criteria or do away with it
completely as the Reform Party in the UK is now campaigning
on.
Risk number two is exploitation of the student visas and
intra-corporate and specialist transfer provisions in Annex
So these pathways are uncapped, and the potential exists
for them to be exploited to bring in more people than is appropriate or
desirable. So I suggest ongoing monitoring
with additional restrictions placed on education providers
or Indian investment and commercial activity if problems are
detected.
Risk three is around the investment provisions.
The FTA contains a so-called aspirational target for
US 20 billion investment into India over the next 15 years.
This is larger than all of New Zealand's current FDI put together,
and it seems unrealistic we would even meet this target.
And although we've been told we only have to promote investment towards it,
India appears to have powers under the agreement to instigate a
government-to-government consultation process and roll back some of the
FDA's trade concessions if it thinks New Zealand is not doing enough to meet the
target.
This poses risks for our firms if they commit to developing markets,
supply chains, investments in India, and then the trade access gets cut
off. So my request
to the committee or to officials is for some consideration about what the New
Zealand government could or should do if India chose to
exercise those powers.
Thank you. That's the end of my submission.
Excellent. Hey, thank you very much. Appreciate that final point too, William.
Thanks for taking the time.
Thank you.
Cheers.

The question really for this committee is not whether there's any benefits because

It's about whether they've actually been

For example, the 20 billion question, it seems as though from
note two of Article 9.2,
that
the 20 billion objective is based on India's growth.
And if we're going by technicality, it doesn't state whether New

So to conclude,
we believe in
free trade. We believe that we can get good agreements.
We do not oppose a trade with China.
Sorry, India.
But a trade agreement should not be judged by whether a deal was signed, whether

It should actually be judged by whether the final bargain is fair for both sides,
and it's the best for both sides, and that's kind of the challenge here.
On the evidence available to us from our analysis, we don't believe it's been
met, and we recommend that it shouldn't be ratified, and it should go back to
the negotiating table if possible.
All right.
